OSINTdefender NASA’s Artemis II mission is set to return to Earth today, following their trip around the moon. According to the released timetable, the Orion crew module will separate from the service module and begin its descent around 7:30 p.m. ET, with the first set of parachutes expected to deploy around 8:00 p.m. ET, following their 3.9 G reentry into Earth’s atmosphere. Due to concerns over the Orion spacecraft’s heat shield, NASA plans to send the craft on a more direct reentry route to limit the time it is exposed to the extreme temperatures of reentry into Earth’s atmosphere. tweet

Open Source Intel The Iranian delegation for nuclear talks with the U.S., led by Ghalibaf, has landed in Islamabad. The delegation arrived on two aircraft, the first flights to depart Iran since the start of the military campaign, with Iranian airspace still closed. The planes were escorted by Pakistani fighter jets. Flares were observed being deployed. The aircraft belong to Iranian airline Mraj (registration EP-AJJ and EP-SSN), departing from Gorgan airport. Mraj provides VIP and charter services to the ayatollah regime. @ItayBlumental tweet

Bush and the 11th Marine Expeditionary Unit (from the USS BOXER assault landing ship) are again heading toward the region; their arrival will likely take more than a week. ⚡️ 🇮🇶 ☀️ 🇮🇶 🇮🇷 ☀️ ☀️ Iraq’s parliament is set to convene on Saturday 11 April to elect a new president, but deep political divisions threaten the session. In Iraq’s Muhasasa (sectarian power-sharing) system, the presidency is the “gatekeeper” — elected first in order to formally designate the prime minister. Traditionally the three top posts are negotiated as a single package, and the current deadlock stems from a fight over who gets the premiership before that gate is opened. Kurdistan Democratic Party leader Masoud Barzani said he will not proceed with any constitutional entitlement before the prime minister candidate is decided. The KDP and the State of Law coalition — led by Nouri al‑Maliki, who is aggressively pushing for a third term despite a reported US “soft veto” — announced a boycott of the session. Behind the boycott is a tactical trade: Maliki has reportedly promised to back a KDP president in exchange for KDP support for his own PM bid later. Several blocs have confirmed attendance: Reconstruction and Development Coalition led by outgoing Iraqi Prime Minister Sudani (who is vying to keep his job against Maliki’s challenge), Alliance of Nation State Forces led by Ammar al‑Hakim, Al‑Sadiqoun Bloc led by Qais Khazali the sanctioned leader of the Khazali Network, the Babylon Movement led by Rayan al‑Kildani the sanctioned militia leader of the Babylon Brigades, and Taqaddum led by influential Sunni leader Mohamed Al‑Halbousi — who recently described Iraq’s past experience with Maliki as “bitter” and signaled his opposition to Maliki’s return. The Kurdish New Generation movement, Kurdish National Stance Movement led by Ali Hama Saleh, the Kurdistan Islamic Union and the Kurdistan Justice Group also said they will attend — a move that challenges the traditional KDP‑PUK duopoly at a moment when the two major Kurdish parties are locked in their own civil war. The longstanding “gentleman’s agreement” that gave the PUK the Baghdad presidency while the KDP kept Erbil has collapsed; the KDP now argues its larger 27‑seat bloc entitles it to the federal presidency too, while the PUK refuses to surrender the post. PDK parliamentary Deputy speaker Farhad Atroushi opposed the session, citing a lack of national consensus. The boycott strategy relies on the “blocking third” — with over 110 MPs staying away, the required two‑thirds quorum of 220 out of 329 members cannot be met, legally preventing the session from starting. Parliamentary Speaker Haibat al‑Halbousi called on political leaders to attend and said he will publish names of absent MPs and the blocs that prevent their members from voting — a desperate “name and shame” tactic to pressure individual lawmakers to break party lines. Presidential candidate Asso Fereydoun said his nomination is conditional on a binding agreement among Kurdish blocs and will withdraw if no such accord is reached — a direct reflection of the fractured Kurdish house. The Sunni Azm coalition said its participation is contingent on consensus or a majority of the Shiite Coordination Framework, effectively handing the final say on the session’s success to the Shiite factions themselves. Chief Justice Faiq Zaidan issued a “code red” warning that the failure to implement constitutional texts puts the judiciary in a “real predicament”, turning the constitution into a “theoretical text” and leading to recurring political crises that undermine state prestige and development. Behind that warning lies the unspoken “nuclear option”: if parliament cannot fulfill its duty, the Federal Supreme Court could rule it negligent and trigger its dissolution, forcing fresh elections that most sitting MPs are desperate to avoid. @wfwitness

📸 Al Jazeera shows heavily damaged AN/FPS-132 early warning radar at Al Udeid Air Base in Qatar, a key U.S. ballistic-missile detection system. The AN/FPS-132 early-warning radar at Al Udeid Air Base in Qatar is a $1.1 billion U.S.-built missile-warning system that detects ballistic missiles up to ~5,000 km and feeds data to regional defenses like THAAD and Patriot.

The U.S. is reinforcing its military presence in the Middle East ahead of upcoming U.S.–Iran negotiations in Pakistan. Fighter and attack aircraft have already arrived. 1,500–2,000 troops from the 82nd Airborne Division may deploy soon. The USS George H.W. Bush carrier strike group is en route from the Atlantic. The USS Boxer and the 11th Marine Expeditionary Unit are also heading to the region from the Pacific. Source: WSJ

WATCH: Qatar’s Al Jazeera shows, for the first time, the moment Iranian missiles struck Ras Laffan Industrial City, Qatar’s gas hub. The attacks caused major direct damage to critical infrastructure and had global repercussions.

The parties agreed to hold a first meeting on Tuesday at the State Department to discuss a ceasefire announcement and the start of negotiations under US auspices. @wfwitness
